description: THIS EVENT IS BEING RESCHEDULED DUE TO WEATHER AND RU OPERATING STATUS FOR 2/24/26. NEW DATE FORTHCOMING. Join the Center for Women in the Arts at Douglass for a workshop led by acclaimed artist Liz Collins! This unique needlepoint workshop invites you to explore the meditative joy of stitching. Collins will guide participants through the myriad of stitches possible within needlepoint and share her distinctive methodology for creating improvisational designs, treating needlepoint as a slow drawing process in response to the grid. All skill levels and forms of stitching are welcome and supported.  Only open to Douglass and Art & Design students. Free supplies and snacks provided! Supplies/snacks are limited – First come, first served. Space is limited. Please arrive on-time. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research. Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. *Liz Collins is the 2025-2026 Estelle Lebowitz & Ann Beneduce-Lebowitz Artist-in-Residence. Her solo exhibition, Shapeshifter, is on view through March 13, 2026 in the Mary H. Dana Women Artists Series Galleries at Douglass Libraries. ___ * Image and Media ReleaseBy attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. All rights to these materials will belong to Rutgers University. Please let our photographer or videographer know if you do not want your image taken at the event.
name: Needlepoint Workshop with artist Liz Collins
organizationName: Academic Programs at Douglass Residential College - DRC

description: What does it mean to survive—intellectually, politically, ecologically, or aesthetically—in a moment defined by crisis? When uncertainty and chaos prevail, those who endure must bend, not break. They must adapt, redirect, stay fluid. How do figures of water help us think survival not as a static state but as a process: precarious, contingent, always at risk of being swept under? Survival and water: two conceptual forces that converge in literary, philosophical, and political discourses across the German tradition. Whether in the form of biblical floods, rising oceans, or mythic rivers—water stages a sight of duality: It is where life and death, healing and destruction, calmness and acceleration are able to meet in a resistant stream of becoming. To survive—especially “with one’s head above water”—is to inhabit the threshold between endurance and submersion, grasping for breath, clarity, meaning. At the same time, survival carries its own paradoxes: Who survives and at what cost? What kinds of knowledge or expression survive catastrophe, exile, war, or erasure—and which are drowned out? Water leaks across genres and disciplines: as metaphor for time and memory, as site of passage and peril, as resource and threat, as a medium of cleansing, forgetting, or border-policing. Meanwhile, survival speaks equally to historical trauma and everyday precarity, to climate catastrophe and cultural memory, to the bodies that wash ashore and those that remain submerged in the archive. This conference seeks contributions that take up the dual themes of survival and water—literally, figuratively, or formally—as critical frameworks. We welcome engagements with water as metaphor, medium, matter, and menace, and with survival as a philosophical problem, a narrative form, or a historical condition. What does it mean to be in over one’s head—psychically, politically, or ecologically? How might “keeping one’s head above water” register both resistance and exhaustion? How are the concepts of water and survival marshaled by thinkers, writers, artists, or regimes? How do literature, film, and theory reckon with flood, immersion, drowning, or breath? What kinds of bodies are asked to stay afloat, and at what cost? Brief: how do we keep our head above water; how do we survive the current?
location: Academic Building West, Room 4052 for Thursday and Room 6051 for Friday
name: HEAD ABOVE WATER: Surviving the Current. Rutgers German Graduate Student Conference

description: ScarletPitch is a university-wide competition for Rutgers-New Brunswick undergraduates to present innovative ideas that tackle global challenges. Whether it’s a social action project, a startup venture, or another big idea, this platform empowers students to showcase their creativity and make an impact! This event is open to all members of the Rutgers community to attend. Join us for a night of great food, fun, camaraderie, and the opportunity to meet and mingle with IDEA’s network of startup founders, mentors, and professionals. You’ll also have the chance to vote for the Audience Choice! Sign-up to attend here:  https://go.rutgers.edu/srl4qtpd  Doors will open at 4:15 pm for registration and networking before the event starting at 5 pm. Observing Ramadan As we come together for an exciting evening, we want to recognize and extend our heartfelt support to those observing Ramadan. We honor the importance of this holy month and the time it represents for spiritual reflection, prayer, and fasting. Our goal is to make ScarletPitch a welcoming and inclusive experience for everyone. If you are observing Ramadan and need any accommodations or support to participate comfortably, please don’t hesitate to reach out. We are happy to work with you and offer the following:  Halal options for those who indicated this dietary option during online registration.  An Interfaith Meditation Room will be available for prayer and reflection on the 1st floor of: Douglass Student Center
name: ScarletPitch 2026

Note that abstract submission deadline is January 30th, 2026.    We are looking for faculty to judge the posters and talks. If you are able to help, please contact Prateeksha Rout as soon as possible. pr398@gsbs.rutgers.edu   Everyone attending the symposium including students, faculty and postdocs must register by March 2nd in order to receive a boxed lunch.    We look forward to seeing you there!   -Your Molecular Biosciences Graduate Student Organization (MBGSO) Board

description: All BME-affiliated undergraduates, graduate students, and postdocs are invited to present their research through either posters or oral presentations. The symposium is open to the entire BME department, including non-BME students conducting research in BME labs. A panel of judges will assess both posters and presentations, and winners will receive a prize! Faculty interested in participating as judges, please reach out to us at rutgersbess@gmail.com to let us know! Please use this Google form to indicate your interest in presenting: https://forms.gle/9mdozEzRJ2NRjXgP8.   To submit an abstract, we will ask for the following: 1. Title, Authors, and Affiliations.2. An abstract (cannot exceed 250 words) that can include the following sections:   - Introduction   - Materials and Methods   - Results (If Applicable)   - Conclusions   Abstracts are due on 03/01/26 at 11:59 pm. All participants are welcome to present their posters, and those chosen for an oral presentation will be notified by the week of 03/08/26.  We hope you will join us to showcase our community's outstanding research, celebrate your achievements, and foster new collaborations and discussions! We will send out further details about the event schedule and logistics at a later date.
name: 7th Annual BESS Symposium

description: CLICK HERE TO REGISTER!   The Rutgers University Honors College is thrilled to present the 1st Annual Heart & Sole 5K at Buccleuch Park!   The race kicks off at 10:30 AM sharp, so come early to grab your bib and free event t-shirt! Pickup opens at 9:00 AM at the Large Pavilion. Every registrant will also earn a finisher medal as they cross the line!   Stick around afterward for our 11:30 AM Award Ceremony, where top finishers will be recognized with exciting awards. Extra prizes will also be raffled off!   All registration fees and donations will support the American Heart Association in honor of American Heart Month. Thank you for helping us make a meaningful impact! Registration is capped at 200 participants, so don’t wait—spots will fill fast!   Additionally, Honors College students will be awarded 2 community service hours upon request!
name: Heart & Sole 5K

description: As a graduate student, teaching assistant, instructor, or friend, you may wonder about academic accommodations and accessibility. The Office of Disability Services is hosting a drop-in afternoon for graduate students interested in learning about the services that ODS provides. Come stop by the Graduate Student Lounge or join via Zoom and meet Coordinator Nychey Michel to discuss accommodations for: hearing, vision, and mobility conditions, learning disabilities, psychological diagnoses (such as anxiety or depression), chronic illnesses, traumatic brain injuries, ADHD, Autism Spectrum Disorder, and more. Bring yourself or bring a friend to ask questions and learn about outreach efforts. Coffee and donuts will be provided while supplies last!   Questions or accommodation requests specific to this event? Please email dsoffice@echo.rutgers.edu, call 848-202-3111, or visit ODS in Lucy Stone Hall, Suite A145, Livingston Campus.
name: Graduate Student Disability Services Drop-In Hours
organizationNames: Graduate Student Life (Department at Rutgers University)
Office of Disability Services (University Department)

description: This co-organized event between Rutgers and Princeton University marks the inaugural meeting of scholars from both institutions focusing on Abya Yala, the Kuna term for the indigenous territory that encompasses what is now known as Latin America.  Taking up the notion of “trans-indigenous” methodologies as proposed by Chadwick Allen – that is, thinking about the indigenous beyond the imposition of colonial borders– our gathering explores connections across indigenous communities throughout the Americas, and invites scholars from across the region to participate in this conversation.  With an interdisciplinary focus, the event brings together topics such as indigenous literature, film, ancestral medicinal practices, and oral testimony to examine how indigenous knowledge systems and traditions circulate and resonate across borders, languages, and communities. We will be joined by guest keynote speaker, poet and professor Odi González (NYU), who will address the complex process of translating oral Quechua literature into written languages, such as Spanish and English. The event will finish with a film screening of Javier Corcuera's Sigo siendo (2013), a documentary which dives into the heterogenous musical expressions of Peru, influenced by local cultures and repertoires from both indigenous and Afro-Peruvian roots. With a focus on migrant subjects in the industrial capital, the film highlights the impossibility of conceiving contemporary indigenous societies in isolation, celebrating the diverse community ties they create as they work to maintain their cultural vibrancy.
name: Trans-Indigenous Abya Yala  1st Dialogue in Indigenous Studies at Rutgers-Princeton University
organizationName: Literary Studies in Spanish Organization (Graduate Organization)

description: Drop-in for an Artful Saturday monthly event hosted by the Center for the Women in the Arts at Douglass and Scarlet Arts RX! Join us for a Spring painting picnic in Jameson courtyard (rain location: Ludwig Center). Artsy door prizes! Bring a friend and a blanket to sit on (if you have one)! Taught by a Scarlet Arts Rx student teaching artist from Rutgers Mason Gross School of the Arts. Free supplies, snacks, and boba provided! Supplies are limited – First come, first served. All skill levels and walk-ins welcome! Foundational art skills you will learn at this event: Painting and referencing nature, Color theory and color relationships. This experience counts towards the Douglass Discovery curriculum requirement. R3: Career/Discovery/Professional Development/Research.Reminder: To receive DRC curriculum credit, you must complete a reflection survey. ___ * Image and Media Release By attending this event, you acknowledge and agree that Rutgers University may photograph, record, or otherwise capture your artwork, image, and/or voice. You grant Rutgers the unrestricted right to use, reproduce, publish, and distribute these sound, still, or moving images in any medium for educational, promotional, advertising, or other purposes aligned with the University’s mission. All rights to these materials will belong to Rutgers University. Please let our photographer or videographer know if you do not want your image taken at the event.
location: Jameson Courtyard / Schonberg Rm, Ludwig (rain location)

description: Come join us on Sunday, April 19, for Haru's biggest event of the semester, Spring 2026 Showcase! Our goal is to share Korean pop culture through the art of dance. This event is open to everyone: general members, families, Rutgers students, and non-Rutgers students! Our showcase is our biggest event, where our members get to showcase a multitude of K-pop performances they’ve been preparing for months for our audience. There are two acts with an intermission, which includes a raffle with a bunch of prizes our audience can partake in and possibly win big! And the best part is that admission is FREE for all!  You don't have to be performing to attend, so come out and support our performers! ♡ Let's cheer each other on and enjoy each other's company! You may also get some sweet treats, snacks, and refreshments from us to enjoy while watching performances 👀 Our showcase is also livestreamed on our YouTube page for audience members who cannot attend in person, but would love to support our performers! If you're watching from online, thank you for supporting Haru Dance ♡ More information on parking will be detailed here later in the semester. Contact us directly on IG: @harudance_official or Email: haru.kdcc@gmail.com if you have any questions! Can't wait to see y'all soon!
location: Cook/Douglass - Trayes Hall Douglass Student Center
name: HARU Spring 2026 Showcase
